title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series Ep 50: Generate Stunning AI Images for Social Media (50+ Free Workflows)
description:
Welcome to Episode 50 of the ComfyUI Tutorial Series! 🎉 To celebrate nearly a year of sharing AI knowledge, I’m giving you over 50 FREE workflows designed for social media image creation — from 2K Instagram visuals to realistic full HD portrait content for YouTube Shorts.

📷 Whether you're curious about how to:
- Achieve Instagram influencer-style image quality,
- Create realistic AI visuals,
- Use ControlNet, LoRA, and upscalers in ComfyUI,
- Or organize your workflows like a pro...

This episode is a goldmine 💎
⚙️ Featuring:
- Flux vs Fluxmania model comparisons
- Exact export settings for 2K, 16:9, 4:5 and more
- Smart denoise tips, Lora integration, and upscale workflow insights

💾 All workflows available for free download on our Discord server! Includes install instructions for easy setup.

Get the workflows and instructions from discord for free
First accept this invite to join the discord server: https://discord.gg/gggpkVgBf3
Then you cand find the workflows in pixaroma-worfklow
transcript: Welcome to episode 50 of our comfy UI tutorial series.  Can you believe it's almost a year since I started the first episode?  So today to celebrate, I have more than 50 free workflows for you.  I've seen a lot of questions on Discord like,  how do you do posts for social media like those Instagram influencers?  How do you create more realistic images?  How do you make exact sizes for social media like 2k images or full HD images for shorts?  I hope today's video will answer all those questions.  So you already know that in comfy UI, you can drag a workflow to the canvas to open it,  or you can go to the Workflow tab and click on Open, then select the workflow you want to open  with the .json extension. That will open the workflow with all the nodes and settings.  You can close that workflow just like you close a document in Photoshop, for example.  I will add all the workflows on Discord.  I'll try to upload them separately so you can download only what you need,  and also all together in a zip archive so you can extract it and have access to all the workflows.  I included a text file with instructions on how to install them for easy access.  If you don't want to search for them on your hard drive all the time,  here's how I like to do it to stay organized.  I copy the folders into the workflows folder.  So I copy both of the folders, but if you only want to use one of the models,  you can copy just that one. Then I go to where comfy UI is installed and look for the folder  called user. Inside that, you find the folder default, and then finally the folder we were looking  for is workflows. I paste all the workflows here, and if you save some of your own,  they should also appear here. Yes, you can organize them into folders and subfolders.  You can rename the folders and workflows to make them easier for you to remember.  Then you need to close comfy UI if it was open and restart it so it can detect those workflows.  Now, if we go to the workflows folder on the left, we have the exact same folder structure.  You can resize the sidebar so you can see all the text if you want, and when you click on a folder,  it expands so you can see the subfolders. As you can see, I created a lot of them for both flux  and flux mania models. Those two models are the ones I currently use. All are tested on an RTX 4090  with 24 gigabytes of V-RAM, but they should work with 12 gigabytes of V-RAM, or even less if you  use a smaller flux model. Why did I select those two? Because flux mania produces the most realistic  results in people, it adds imperfections, and makes images more realistic, and it's also uncensored.  Flux I use because I can run it with Laura trained on Flux Dev, and I get more vivid colors that  work well for illustrations. It also has fewer imperfections since it doesn't try to be realistic.  Flux Mania is an FP8 model, so from what I've heard, it doesn't seem to work on Mac. Both have  advantages and disadvantages, but with these two models, I can generate most of the images I need.  I created a workflow for the most popular ratios on social media. There's 2K landscape so you can  create AI images in a 16-9 ratio for YouTube and other social media that works for landscape videos,  YouTube thumbnails, or posts on X and Facebook. There's full HD 9-16 ratio for those who need images  for shorts and reels. Instagram portrait with a 4-5 ratio, and of course a square ratio that works  for most social media posts. Flux likes to work with multiples of 832 or 64 for width and height,  so when you want to do an exact size for social media, you'll see you often can't do that exact  size. Even if you put in those exact sizes, the image quality usually isn't great, so I tried to find  the best settings and nodes to get exactly the size you need for social media. Let's see what workflows  we have in each folder. In the basic text to image and image to image folders, we have workflows that  do just that. Simple workflows with upscalers. Here's how I name the workflows. I first started with  the model, so it's either flux or flux mania, then I added whether it's a text to image workflow,  which means you input just text and get an image, or it can be an image to image workflow,  where you input your own image and a prompt to get another image. After that, I added some extra  words, depending on what extra features it has, like upscaler, collage, control net, Laura,  so you know what it contains. I included the max image size you get after upscaling in pixels,  and also the ratio, like one to one, or 16 to nine, and so on. I hope the naming makes sense to  you, but you can rename them however you want. Moving to the next folder, we have a few control  network flows and also one with control net and Laura. Then in the Laura folder, we have text to  image workflows with Laura and an upscaler with Laura included so you can upscale images that were  made with a certain Laura. The last one is just an upscaler, so you can upload an image and get a  bigger version. It's for AI images with small sizes. If the image is bigger than 1024 pixels,  it's scaled down so the AI can handle it and then upscaled. Let's say I want to do a 2K landscape  image for my YouTube. If I try to run the workflow again, nothing happens because I set the seed to be fixed.  If you want to change it, click on the arrow, or just use a different number. So if you don't like the image, change the seed to a different number and hit run.  This is the result. It has some imperfections on the face, but sometimes that can make it look more  real. I can adjust the denoise level from here. I use 0.7 for flux mania because if it's too high,  it adds too many imperfections. The upscaler takes around 100 seconds on my PC, so in total with generation  and upscaling, it's about two minutes to get a 2k image. In the output folder, you have the  original image and also the upscaled image. So you can use whichever one you need. This was the  first upscale, and this was the second one. If I switch between before and after, you can see  small differences. If I increase the denoise value, the differences get bigger, but the woman will  look less similar to the original image. Another problem Flux Mania has that Flux doesn't is this line  you sometimes get on the right side. It can be easily fixed, but it's something to be aware of because  it happens sometimes. This time, the image to image work  so we can upload an image and get a variation of it. It's different, but pretty similar. If I want it to be  more different, I increase the denoise value. For example, if I use 0.75, it should have more freedom  to change things and I got this image. See how different it is, but it still keeps something from  the original composition and colors. What I don't like is that it looks more like an illustration  while the original was more like a photo, but that depends on the prompt and the model. We know  Flux Mania can do realistic images, so we need to change the prompt. I could spend a minute trying to  improve the prompt, but a faster way for me is to just use ChatGPT. At 0.8, it still looks fine, but let's go to the max of  0.95 to see what happens. We get a lot of details, but because it's using tiling, now there's another  house here, mushrooms everywhere. Even on the tree, everything has details that shouldn't be there,  so don't go too high with the D noise value, especially for the Flux Mania model. Flux works a  little better since it doesn't add as many details. Here are some examples where the background  was simple, and you can see that high D noise and the tiling settings gave me random houses in  the background. Same for this image. I got another door here, so just something to keep in mind,  with high D noise, you get more errors. 0.75 or 0.7 is a good starting point. For those who get an  error with allocation on device, the error you usually get when you don't have enough video memory,  you can sometimes fix it from here. For cards like the RTX 4090, you can go even higher,  like 1024 pixels for tile width and height.
